flashcards.back: If PÔåÆQ is true and P is true, then Q must be true. Formally: (PÔåÆQ) Ôêº P ÔçÆ Q
If PÔåÆQ is true and Q is false, then P must be false. Formally: (PÔåÆQ) Ôêº ┬¼Q ÔçÆ ┬¼P
If PÔåÆQ and QÔåÆR are true, then PÔåÆR is true. Formally: (PÔåÆQ) Ôêº (QÔåÆR) ÔçÆ (PÔåÆR)
If PÔê¿Q is true and ┬¼P is true, then Q must be true. Formally: (PÔê¿Q) Ôêº ┬¼P ÔçÆ Q
From P, we can infer PÔê¿Q. Adding any proposition to a disjunction preserves truth.
From PÔêºQ, we can infer P (or Q). A conjunction implies each of its conjuncts.
From P and Q, we can infer PÔêºQ. Both propositions must be true for the conjunction to be true.
From (PÔåÆQ) Ôêº (RÔåÆS) and PÔê¿R, we can infer QÔê¿S. Both implications lead to a disjunction.
From (PÔåÆQ) Ôêº (RÔåÆS) and ┬¼QÔê¿┬¼S, we can infer ┬¼PÔê¿┬¼R. The negation of consequences implies negation of antecedents.
From (PÔê¿Q) and (┬¼PÔê¿R), we can infer QÔê¿R. A fundamental rule used in automated theorem proving.

flashcards.front: Define a Valid argument in propositional logic
flashcards.back: An argument is valid if whenever all premises are true, the conclusion is also true. The truth of premises guarantees truth of conclusion.
An argument is sound if it is valid AND all its premises are actually true in the real world.
A proposition that is always true, regardless of truth values of its components. Example: PÔê¿┬¼P (Law of Excluded Middle)
A proposition that is always false. Example: PÔêº┬¼P (Law of Non-Contradiction)
Two propositions are logically equivalent if they have the same truth value in all possible interpretations. Denoted as PÔëíQ.
